name: Spotify's Mood/Audio Feature Taxonomy
core_claim: Spotify's audio-feature taxonomy (inherited from The Echo Nest, which Spotify acquired in 2014) claims that the affective character and functional fit of a piece of music can be reduced to a small set of continuous, machine-extractable scalar features, each scored 0.0-1.0 per track. The headline 'mood' claim is that musical positiveness ('valence') and perceived intensity/activity ('energy') are the two dominant affective dimensions, supplemented by function-oriented features such as danceability (suitability for dancing), and content/production descriptors such as acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness and speechiness. The implicit and commercially proven thesis is that 'vibe' — the thing listeners actually pick on ('something upbeat', 'something chill', 'sad music') — is operationalisable as a position in a low-dimensional feature space computed directly from the audio signal, and that this space is good enough to drive playlisting, radio seeding, mood-based recommendation and recommendation-by-example at planetary scale. It is the most widely deployed and commercially validated affective-feel taxonomy in existence, even though the per-feature algorithms are proprietary and never published. Note: as of 2026 this taxonomy is largely of historical/conceptual relevance — Spotify deprecated public API access to audio-features/audio-analysis on 27 November 2024.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Hybrid, but anchored experientially on the dimensions that matter for 'vibe'. Two of the headline features are purely experiential/affective: valence (felt positiveness — happy/cheerful vs sad/angry) and energy (felt intensity and activity). These map almost exactly onto the valence-arousal pair of Russell's circumplex and are about how the music FEELS, not what it is about. Danceability is FUNCTIONAL/behavioural — it predicts a use (dancing) rather than a felt quality or a topic, occupying a 'what is it good for' dimension distinct from both descriptive and affective axes. The remaining features (acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness, speechiness, plus the structural tempo/key/mode/loudness/time-signature) are DESCRIPTIVE/production-property: they describe objective sonic characteristics of the recording, not its felt affect. So the taxonomy spans three levels — experiential (valence, energy), functional (danceability), and descriptive/sonic (the rest) — but its claim to have 'solved vibe' rests on the experiential pair. Crucially, music has no narrative content layer, so unlike narrative frameworks there is no 'what it is ABOUT' topical dimension at all (lyrics aside); the descriptive features describe the SOUND, not a subject.
key_dimensions: The canonical 13-field per-track feature vector returned by the (now-deprecated) Spotify audio-features endpoint: (1) VALENCE 0.0-1.0 — musical positiveness (high = happy/cheerful/euphoric, low = sad/depressed/angry); (2) ENERGY 0.0-1.0 — perceptual intensity and activity, derived from dynamic range, perceived loudness, timbre, onset rate, general entropy (death metal high, Bach prelude low); (3) DANCEABILITY 0.0-1.0 — suitability for dancing from tempo, rhythm stability, beat strength, regularity; (4) ACOUSTICNESS 0.0-1.0 — confidence the track is acoustic; (5) INSTRUMENTALNESS 0.0-1.0 — confidence the track has no vocals; (6) LIVENESS 0.0-1.0 — probability the track was performed live (audience presence); (7) SPEECHINESS 0.0-1.0 — presence of spoken words; (8) LOUDNESS in dB (typically -60 to 0); (9) TEMPO in BPM; (10) KEY (pitch class 0-11); (11) MODE (major=1, minor=0); (12) TIME_SIGNATURE; (13) DURATION_MS. The affective core for 'mood' purposes is the valence x energy plane; danceability and acousticness are the next most semantically loaded for 'vibe'. All features are continuous (except key/mode/time-signature) and orthogonal-ish per-track scalars with no temporal/arc structure — one summary number per whole track.
cross_cultural_validity: Effectively untested in any published, controlled cross-cultural sense, and structurally biased toward Western popular-music norms. The underlying Echo Nest models were trained on (predominantly Western, commercially distributed) catalogue audio, and the affective labels ('happy', 'sad', major=positive, minor=negative) encode Western music-theory and Western emotion-in-music conventions that are known not to generalise — e.g. the major=happy / minor=sad association is culturally learned and weak or absent in some non-Western traditions, and rhythmic conceptions of 'danceability' are culturally specific. Because the algorithms are proprietary and unpublished, there is no transparency to audit cultural bias, and Spotify has published no cross-cultural validation. The features ARE applied globally across Spotify's catalogue, but global DEPLOYMENT is not the same as cross-cultural VALIDITY: the features will be computed for, e.g., gamelan, qawwali, or West African polyrhythmic music, but whether the valence/energy/danceability scores mean the same thing to listeners in those traditions is unknown and probably violated at the margins. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Mixed and asymmetric. (a) COMMERCIAL/computational grounding is enormous and real: the features have driven Spotify's recommendation, radio and playlisting for over a decade across hundreds of millions of users — the largest-scale de facto validation of any affective-feel taxonomy by sheer downstream usage, and they demonstrably support useful retrieval ('upbeat', 'chill', 'workout', 'sad'). (b) ACADEMIC validation against human perception is sparse, recent, and only partly favourable. The most direct study (Greer et al. / 'Validating Spotify's Valence, Energy, and Danceability', PsyArXiv/OSF preprint, Sept 2025; 244 participants rating 40 excerpts) found Spotify's energy correlates reasonably well with human arousal ratings, valence correlates moderately with human positivity ratings, and danceability is the weakest/most divergent from human judgement — confirming the features are useful but imperfect proxies for perceived emotion. (c) TRANSPARENCY is poor: the per-feature algorithms are proprietary and never published, so the construct validity of 'valence' in particular cannot be independently audited — community reverse-engineering projects (e.g. 'Dissecting Spotify Valence') exist precisely because Spotify never documented the derivation. To validate LLM-extracted analogues you would need: human valence/energy/danceability ratings of a title/track set as ground truth, inter-rater reliability, and ideally downstream choice/satisfaction data — and you would have to treat Spotify's own scores as a noisy reference, not gold-standard truth.
operationalisability: This taxonomy is the gold standard for operationalisability WITHIN ITS NATIVE MODALITY (audio): the features are computed automatically from the waveform by a trained model, requiring no human annotation — that automation is exactly why it scaled. The relevant question for HelpME2C is operationalisability of the ANALOGOUS dimensions from a TV/anime synopsis by an LLM, which is a very different and harder problem because (i) there is no audio signal, only text, and (ii) the extraction unit differs. The two affective features that transfer conceptually — valence and energy — are coarsely extractable from a synopsis + genre + tone (overall positive/negative tone; calm vs intense/fast pacing), with the same failure modes already catalogued for Russell's circumplex: conflating subject-matter valence with felt valence, genre-label anchoring, flattening of bittersweet/mixed valence, and ignoring affective TRAJECTORY (Spotify's one-number-per-track summary is itself a known limitation — it has no arc representation). Danceability and the sonic features (acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness, speechiness, tempo, key, mode, loudness) DO NOT transfer to narrative at all — they are properties of an audio recording with no synopsis analogue. Extraction unit: Spotify uses whole-track; for HelpME2C the natural unit is whole-work, with the same loss of dynamics. Failure modes for LLM extraction mirror the circumplex case; additionally, the proprietary, undocumented nature of Spotify's definitions means there is no reliable spec to extract AGAINST.
motivation_served: Primarily HEDONIC and MOOD-REGULATORY, with a strong FUNCTIONAL/contextual overlay. Valence and energy map directly onto mood repair and arousal regulation (pick high-valence to lift mood, low-energy to calm down, high-energy to get hyped) — the affective substrate of mood-management/selective-exposure. Danceability and the implicit activity-fit framing (workout, focus, party, sleep playlists built from these features) serve CONTEXTUAL/situational needs — matching music to an activity rather than a feeling — a dimension that is largely absent from narrative-affect frameworks but very relevant to 'what should we watch tonight'. It does not address eudaimonic appreciation, meaning, identity, or social-connection motivations directly (music's eudaimonic/being-moved appeal is invisible to these features, as is the appreciation of sad music). For GROUP viewing the model is tractable in the same way the circumplex is: two people's current/desired valence-energy targets are points in a shared low-dimensional space, so a joint pick can minimise aggregate distance; danceability's analogue — shared ACTIVITY/context fit ('do we both want something intense or something we can half-watch') — is an additional, separable group constraint worth soliciting per session.
receiver_dependence: Strongly WORK-ALONE in design — and that is the model's defining commercial bet. Every feature is a per-track property computed from the audio with no reference to any listener, so valence, energy, danceability etc. are ABSOLUTE work attributes by construction. This is what made the system scalable (one score per track, reused for everyone). But the bet is leaky: the felt valence/energy of a track genuinely depends on listener appraisal and familiarity (the 2025 validation study shows only moderate agreement with human ratings), so the 'absolute' scores are better understood as a consensus estimate that washes out inter-listener variance. The DESIRABILITY of any feature value is, as with the circumplex, wholly RELATIONAL — it depends on the listener's current mood, desired mood, and activity context, none of which live in the track features. So the taxonomy yields quasi-absolute item coordinates (valence, energy, danceability) plus a relational utility (distance to each viewer's transient target). For HelpME2C this reinforces the architecture suggested by Russell/Mood-Management: store stable per-title affective estimates separately from a transient per-session, per-person mood + context input layer.
blind_spots: Blind to ALL semantic content and meaning (it describes sound/feel, not subject or theme) — for narrative this is total, since the entire thematic layer HelpME2C cares about has no audio-feature analogue. Within affect it shares the circumplex's blind spots: it cannot represent MIXED/bittersweet valence (one bipolar positiveness scalar), collapses qualitatively distinct emotions sharing coordinates, and summarises a temporal experience as a STATIC whole-track number, losing arc/build/release/catharsis. It is blind to eudaimonic/being-moved appeal (sad-music enjoyment is mispredicted by a 'maximise valence' reading). It encodes Western music-theory priors (major=positive) as if universal. Its valence construct is OPAQUE and unvalidated by Spotify itself — a black box that community projects have repeatedly tried and failed to fully reverse-engineer. Danceability is the weakest feature against human judgement. It ignores context, social setting, lyrics/meaning, and listener identity. And — operationally critical for anyone relying on it — it is now DEPRECATED: Spotify removed third-party API access to audio-features and audio-analysis on 27 November 2024, so the taxonomy is no longer a live data source for new applications, only a conceptual reference.
vocabulary_implications: Spotify's taxonomy does NOT supply narrative theme slugs and must not be merged into the content theme vocabulary — like Russell's circumplex and Mood Management, its affective scalars belong in a SEPARATE, lightweight, per-title affective/texture layer. It strongly REINFORCES (rather than newly proposes) the two-axis affective layer already recommended from Russell and Mood Management: adopt (1) valence (negative / mixed / positive) for overall felt emotional tone, explicitly distinct from subject-matter darkness, and (2) energy/arousal (low / medium / high) for intensity/pacing — note Spotify validates the SAME pair from a completely different (industrial, audio) lineage, which is convergent evidence the two axes are the right minimal set. The genuinely NEW contribution is the FUNCTIONAL/contextual dimension embodied by danceability and activity-based playlisting: propose adding a per-title 'engagement-mode' or 'attention-demand' axis (e.g. lean-in / lean-back, or active-watch vs background/comfort-watch) capturing how the work is best consumed — this is the narrative analogue of 'good for working out / focusing / unwinding' and is highly relevant to a two-person 'what tonight' decision. Extractability flags: valence and energy are coarsely extractable from synopsis+genre+tone (with the circumplex failure modes); engagement-mode is PARTIALLY extractable from synopsis (pacing, density, genre conventions) but better signalled by runtime/format/episodic-structure metadata than by synopsis text alone — flag as needing supplementary structured metadata. The sonic features (acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness, speechiness, tempo, key, mode) have NO narrative analogue — do not attempt to port them. Also flag the meta-lesson: scaled 'vibe' worked because features were AUTO-EXTRACTED per item and stored as reusable per-item scalars, then combined with transient per-user context at query time — the architecture HelpME2C should mirror.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Orthogonal to the content slugs (found-family, power-corrupts, mystery-investigation, etc. have no audio-feature analogue and are untouched) but a strong external validator of the TEXTURE/tone layer and a prompt to add a functional/engagement axis. It VALIDATES the same texture slugs the circumplex does, via the valence/energy mapping: slow-burn, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death imply LOW energy; absurdist-comedy, dark-comedy imply positive-leaning felt valence delivered despite grim subject (Spotify's valence concept — felt positiveness, not subject positiveness — sharpens exactly this distinction); existential-horror/cosmic-horror/folk-horror imply negative valence but split on energy (cosmic/folk dread = low-energy unease, jump-scare horror = high-energy), a split the current single 'horror' texture grouping blurs. It suggests these texture slugs are MISALIGNED only in leaving valence/energy implicit — the fix is to attach explicit valence + energy values, not relabel. The clearest GAP it reveals beyond the circumplex finding is the FUNCTIONAL/engagement-mode axis: nothing in the current vocabulary captures lean-in vs lean-back / active vs background / comfort-watch suitability, which is the narrative analogue of danceability's 'fit for a use' and a real input to group choice. It does NOT motivate adding or removing any content theme slug. It also reveals a missing 'mixed/bittersweet' valence value (Spotify's bipolar valence is itself a known weakness) and the absence of any transient per-session viewer-mood/context mechanism — both already flagged by adjacent frameworks and reinforced here.

Spotify for Developers blog, 'Introducing some changes to our Web API', 27 Nov 2024 (deprecation of audio-features/audio-analysis/recommendations) https://developer.spotify.com/blog/2024-11-27-changes-to-the-web-api [free]. Greer et al. (2025) 'Validating Spotify's Valence, Energy, and Danceability Audio Features for Music Psychology Research', PsyArXiv/OSF preprint, DOI 10.31234/osf.io/8gfzw https://sciety.org/articles/activity/10.31234/osf.io/8gfzw_v2 and https://www.researchgate.net/publication/395985412 [free preprint]. The Echo Nest (acquired by Spotify 2014) — origin of the audio-feature/valence models; algorithms unpublished/proprietary. Community reverse-engineering: 'Dissecting Spotify Valence' https://github.com/panagiotisgia/Assignment_Dissecting_Spotify_Valence [free]. Greenberg & Rentfrow / Saarikallio et al., PLOS One (2022) 'Music we move to: Spotify audio features and reasons for listening' https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9522267/ [free]. Industry commentary on deprecation: Music Ally https://musically.com/2024/11/28/spotify-removes-features-from-web-api-citing-security-issues/ [free]; Digital Music News https://www.digitalmusicnews.com/2024/12/01/spotify-tightens-api-access-removes-several-data-points/ [free].
